data.title: Sixth Street Specialty Lending targets 11–11.5% ROE for 2026 as it expands investment flexibility with new joint venture
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Sixth Street Specialty Lending, Inc. (TSLX) Q4 2025

Management View

  • CEO Robert Stanley opened his first earnings call as CEO by highlighting the platform's continued strength, discipline amid a dynamic environment, and the appointment of Ross Bruck as Managing Director and Head of Investment Strategy. Stanley stated, “Ross brings a unique perspective that bridges complex asset level underwriting with a strategic lens on market opportunity. His appointment reflects our commitment to elevating our internal talent to drive disciplined investment decisions.”
  • Stanley reported adjusted net investment income of $0.52 per share and adjusted net income of $0.30 per share for the quarter, with annualized operating ROE of 12% and net income ROE of 7%. He emphasized, “Adjusted net investment income of $0.52 per share exceeded our base dividend of $0.46 per share, providing base dividend coverage of 113%.”
  • The company continued its focus on the durability of business models, especially in enterprise software and AI impacts, noting, “We are taking a forward-looking approach in how we underwrite and manage risk. Long-time followers will know that our team has been investing in technology-related businesses for more than 2 decades, and we've navigated multiple periods of significant change.”
  • Stanley described the core portfolio as demonstrating strong credit statistics with consistent revenue and EBITDA growth, and noted a stable weighted average LTV of approximately 41%. He also disclosed that enterprise software exposure is about 40% of the total portfolio by fair value.
  • The Board approved a base quarterly dividend of $0.46 per share and a supplemental dividend of $0.01 per share for Q4.
  • Ross Bruck noted, “Our base case is that the investment environment for 2026 will be characterized by the continued imbalance between the supply of private capital and the demand for financing, resulting in sustained levels of competition and tight spreads for regular way on-the-run transactions.”
  • Bruck reported $242.4 million in commitments and $196.7 million in fundings in Q4, with 97% of investments in First Lien loans and 5 new investments across 4 industries.
  • CFO Ian Simmonds commented, “In Q4, we generated net investment income per share of $0.53, resulting in full year net investment income per share of $2.23.”
  • Simmonds highlighted the formation of Structured Credit Partners (SCP), a joint venture with Carlyle Group BDCs: “We believe SCP will generate returns in the mid-teens on capital invested, which will be accretive to our overall asset level yields. SLX's total commitment to the joint venture is $200 million.”

Outlook

  • Simmonds stated, “Based on our model, which incorporates the forward curve reflects leverage in the middle of our target range and assumes spreads on new investments remain broadly stable, we expect to target a return on equity on net investment income for 2026 of 11% to 11.5%.”
  • The company expects adjusted net investment income per share for 2026 to be in the range of $1.87 to $1.95 using year-end book value per share of $16.97, with $1.21 per share of spillover income at year-end.
  • Management reiterated confidence in dividend coverage and the earnings power of the portfolio, with the anticipated JV returns providing additional support.

Financial Results

  • Net asset value per share at year-end was $16.98, compared to $17.11 in Q3 and $17.09 at year-end 2024 after supplemental dividends.
  • Total investments were $3.3 billion, principal debt outstanding was $1.8 billion, and net assets were $1.6 billion.
  • Debt-to-equity ratio at year-end was 1.1x, down from 1.15x in the prior quarter; average debt-to-equity ratio increased to 1.17x.
  • Total investment income for Q4 was $108.2 million, with interest and dividend income of $95.5 million and other fees of $10.9 million.
  • Weighted average yield on debt and income-producing securities at amortized cost decreased from 11.7% to 11.3% quarter-over-quarter.
  • Non-accruals remained unchanged at 0.6% by fair value.

Q&A

  • Brian McKenna, Citizens JMP Securities: Asked about portfolio turnover since 2022 and the evolution of deployment opportunities around AI. Stanley responded that about 20–25% of NAV is pre-2022 vintages, with most of the portfolio originated post rate hiking cycle. He explained the thematic approach: “Themes rotate often. 18 to 24 months is the general gestation period of a theme, and we're constantly rotating across the platform...those are the ones that I talked about that have the strong moats.”
  • Finian O'Shea, Wells Fargo Securities: Inquired about the SCP JV structure and spillover income implications. Bruck clarified, “These will be broadly syndicated loan CLOs. We don't anticipate the CLOs holding private credit either originated by us or third parties.” Simmonds addressed spillover, noting the ramp-up will be gradual and the framework will help manage stability in NAV.
  • Arren Cyganovich, Truist Securities: Asked about the investment pipeline and unrealized losses. Stanley said, “I think the pipeline is decent. We had good activity in Q4 as you saw a pickup in M&A activity.” Simmonds detailed the losses were primarily due to spread movements and specific reversals.
  • Kenneth Lee, RBC Capital Markets: Sought more on SCP motivations and spread dynamics. Bruck said, “We think the risk return generated by fee-free CLO equity is really attractive within a portfolio context for SLX.” Stanley noted spreads have been stable and does not anticipate major changes soon.
  • Paul Johnson, KBW: Queried the 40% software exposure trend. Stanley stated the exposure “has come down marginally over the past couple of years, in part because we were seeing a decline in unit economics across the software space post the COVID pull-through of demand.”

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts focused on the sustainability of spreads, impact of AI on portfolio durability, pipeline evolution, SCP JV implications, and the company’s ability to capitalize on dislocations. The tone was neutral, with probing on new ventures and risk management but no overt skepticism.
  • Management's tone during prepared remarks was confident and disciplined, emphasizing risk management, thematic investing, and flexibility. During Q&A, the tone remained steady and constructive, with Stanley and Simmonds providing detailed explanations without defensive language.
  • Compared to the previous quarter, management’s tone shifted from transition and succession planning to a focus on strategy execution and forward-looking risk assessment, while analysts’ tone remained neutral and consistently focused on operational details and strategic moves.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance was introduced for 2026 targeting 11–11.5% ROE, while the prior quarter focused on 2025 performance and did not give explicit 2026 targets.
  • Strategic focus expanded to include the SCP JV with Carlyle, aimed at enhancing investment flexibility and returns, whereas the previous call discussed CLO liabilities as a tactical deployment of capital.
  • Analysts’ questions this quarter centered more on new ventures (SCP JV), AI impacts, and the detailed composition of software exposure, while the previous quarter focused heavily on leadership transitions and capital allocation philosophy.
  • Key metrics such as adjusted net investment income per share and NAV per share remained stable quarter-over-quarter, with a slight decrease in NAV and a modest decline in total investment income.
  • Management’s confidence in navigating market volatility and leveraging thematic investing was reinforced, contrasting with the prior quarter’s emphasis on continuity and transition.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management cited sustained competition, tight spreads, and macroeconomic uncertainty as ongoing challenges.
  • The SCP JV’s ramp-up and its impact on spillover income will be monitored to avoid NAV instability.
  • Exposure to enterprise software and the potential for AI-driven disruption were addressed, with management stressing a focus on durable business models and maintaining conservative lending standards.
  • Analysts raised concerns about unrealized losses, sector dislocations, and spread sustainability, which management addressed by detailing risk management frameworks and portfolio positioning.

Final Takeaway

Sixth Street Specialty Lending enters 2026 with a clear focus on disciplined asset selection, robust liquidity, and strategic flexibility, highlighted by the launch of a major joint venture and a targeted ROE of 11–11.5%. With a strong portfolio foundation, prudent risk management, and a forward-looking approach to market opportunities—particularly in technology and structured credit—the company signals confidence in its ability to sustain earnings, cover dividends, and capitalize on evolving credit market dynamics.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Tesla's U.S. auto market share improves even as sales drop by double digits again
data.description:
Tesla (TSLA) saw another electric vehicle sales decline in the U.S. during January, according to state registration data.

U.S. sales for the Austin-based company were estimated to be down 17% for the month, which extends a slump from last year and was magnified by the expiration of the federal tax incentive for EV purchases. 

Despite the soft month, Tesla (TSLA) may have picked up market share because total EV sales are estimated to have fallen over 20% in January. Tesla (TSLA) has cut the prices on key models in the U.S. to stoke demand, while other competitors have de-emphasized their EV programs as they look to boost profitability.

Looking back, Tesla (TSLA) delivered about 1.64M vehicles in 2025, which marked an 8.6% decline from the 2024 tally and its second straight annual drop after 2023’s roughly 1.81M deliveries. After years of rapid expansion, including 38% year‑over‑year growth in 2023, Tesla (TSLA) deliveries essentially flattened in 2024 before contracting in 2025 as softer global EV demand, increased competition, and internal model changeovers weighed on volume.

Shares of Tesla (TSLA) are down just over 6% on a year-to-date basis. The focus by investors on Tesla's (TSLA) AI, autonomy, and Elon Musk's other related companies has shifted the narrative further away from just tracking auto deliveries growth.

data.title: Most and least shorted REIT stocks with over $2B market cap
data.description:
Is a REIT revival on its way? The answer could be a 'Yes'. 

Despite the recent AI-related disruption across real estate services stocks, the State Street Real Estate Select Sector SPDR ETF (XLRE) comfortably outperforms the S&P 500 index year-to-date. XLRE has advanced 6.25%, compared to the broader SP500's negative 0.19% return.

"Economic resilience and a measured path toward lower policy rates are helping to stabilize financing conditions and improve visibility for property cash flows," said Seeking Alpha author Invesco US.

"Independent sector outlooks point to balanced supply and demand and highlight areas of structural strength such as data infrastructure as well as steady trends in housing and logistics," according to Invesco US.

"This environment reduces oversupply risk and supports rent growth across many segments as development pipelines remain restrained," added the author.

With optimism surrounding REIT stocks, shorting activity has also changed its course in about a month's time.

Short interest is the percentage of shares outstanding that have been sold short but have not yet been covered or closed out. Extremely high short interest shows investors are very pessimistic.

Seeking Alpha has compiled a list of the most and least shorted REIT stocks (with a market cap over $2B) in the current market scenario.

Most shorted stocks:

  • NETSTREIT (NTST) - 18.69% of shares outstanding
  • Park Hotels & Resorts (PK) - 16.90% of shares outstanding
  • Acadia Realty Trust (AKR) - 15.81% of shares outstanding
  • Douglas Emmett (DEI) - 15.61% of shares outstanding
  • SL Green Realty (SLG) - 12.70% of shares outstanding

Least shorted stocks:

  • American Tower (AMT) - 1.41% of shares outstanding
  • Prologis (PLD) - 1.47% of shares outstanding
  • Simon Property Group (SPG) - 1.55% of shares outstanding
  • Sun Communities (SUI) - 1.59% of shares outstanding
  • Crown Castle (CCI) - 1.86% of shares outstanding

data.title: The top-performing pharma and biotech stocks as group sees resurgence
data.description:
Pharmaceutical (PJP), (XPH), (PPH) and biotechnology stocks (XBI), (BBH) have officially returned to favor on Wall Street, according to Michael Yee, global head of biotechnology equity research at UBS.

In an interview with CNBC, Yee highlighted a renewed investor appetite for beaten-down healthcare names, even as individual companies like Moderna (MRNA) navigate significant headwinds including regulatory setbacks and substantial cash burn exceeding $2B annually.

“Pharma and biotech are back,” Yee said. “The investor base out there on Wall Street continues to want to come back to some of these names that have been beaten down and hated.”

He noted that the sector has reacted positively to recent developments, including favorable pricing deals signed by the Trump administration.

Moderna’s stock (MRNA) has surged in recent months, driven largely by short covering and optimism around the company’s pipeline turnaround. However, Yee expressed caution, pointing to the FDA’s recent rejection of Moderna’s flu vaccine application as a sign of broader regulatory challenges. The company’s revenues remain a fraction of their 2022 peak during the height of COVID-19 vaccine demand.

A major catalyst on the horizon is Moderna’s (MRNA) cancer vaccine data readout later this year, developed in partnership with Merck (MRK).

Yee said there is a “good chance it will be positive,” potentially generating around $1B in revenue. Still, he noted that such results alone would not be enough to offset the company’s ongoing losses. “They’re really in the business of conserving cash right now. I think they need to pivot to other areas,” he said.

Beyond Moderna, Yee identified several “comeback stories” that he finds compelling, including Merck (MRK), Bristol Myers Squibb (BMY), Amgen (AMGN), and Gilead (GILD).

He described these stocks as cheap relative to their potential and noted they are beginning to break out. Eli Lilly (LLY) remains a top pick in the GLP-1 space, where Yee expects the company to beat and raise its guidance.

The broader political landscape continues to cast uncertainty over vaccine-focused companies. Yee suggested that firms like Moderna (MRNA) may need to shift their focus away from respiratory vaccines toward rare diseases and other applications of their technology to navigate the current environment successfully.

Top-performing biotech and pharma stocks:

  1. Climb Bio, Inc. (CLYM) +11.76%
  2. Cellectis S.A. (CLLS) +9.60%
  3. Vertex Pharmaceuticals Incorporated (VRTX) +7.35%
  4. Foghorn Therapeutics Inc. (FHTX) +6.46%
  5. Alto Neuroscience, Inc. (ANRO) +5.90%
  6. Lyell Immunopharma, Inc. (LYEL) +5.71%
  7. ADC Therapeutics SA (ADCT) +4.98%
  8. Vext Science Inc. (VEXTF) +4.67%
  9. Halozyme Therapeutics, Inc. (HALO) +4.63%
  10. Fortress Biotech, Inc. (FBIO) +4.39%
  11. Septerna, Inc. (SEPN) +4.35%
  12. Oramed Pharmaceuticals Inc. (ORMP) +4.10%
  13. Sera Prognostics, Inc. (SERA) +4.04%
  14. Swedish Orphan Biovitrum AB (publ) (SWOBY) +3.97%
  15. BioAge Labs, Inc. (BIOA) +3.76%

data.title: Traders bet on gradual Fed easing in 2026 as March hold looks likely
data.description:
Investors are closely reassessing the outlook for Federal Reserve policy in 2026 following the latest batch of labor market and inflation data, which has sharpened the debate over how much easing—if any—may unfold this year.

Prediction market Kalshi currently reflects expectations for a moderate easing cycle. The most likely outcome priced by traders is exactly three rate cuts in 2026, with a 30% probability. That is followed by two cuts at 25% and four cuts at 15%. Smaller probabilities are assigned to one cut at 14% or no cuts at all at 9%, while more aggressive easing scenarios—five, six, or seven cuts—collectively command relatively limited odds of under 10%. 

In the near term, however, policy is widely expected to remain on hold. According to the CME FedWatch Tool, markets assign roughly a 90% probability that the Federal Reserve will leave the target federal funds rate unchanged at the upcoming March 18 meeting. 

The current policy band stands at 3.50% - 3.75%. Taken together, market pricing suggests investors anticipate patience from policymakers in the coming months, followed by a gradual and measured easing path later in the year. 

Market Tracking ETFs: (DIA), (DDM), (DOG), (DXD), (SDOW), (SPY), (VOO), (IVV), (RSP), (SSO), (UPRO), (SH), (SDS), (SPXU), (QQQ), (QQQM), (TQQQ), (QID), and (SQQQ). 

Fixed Income ETFs: (TLT), (TLH), (IEF), (IEI), (SHY), (SGOV), (SCHO), (BIL), (AGG), (BND), (VCIT), (MUB), (MBB), (JNK), (LQD), (HYG), (VTIP), (TIP), (SCHP), (STIP), (TIPX), (SPIP), (WIP), (GTIP), (LQDI), and (RINF).

data.title: Hims & Hers short interest climbs amid legal risk related to weight loss pill
data.description:
Short interest in Hims & Hers Health (HIMS) has spiked as shares of the telehealth platform plunged after Novo Nordisk (NVO) sued the company, challenging its now-withdrawn copycat version of the Wegovy weight loss pill originally developed by the Danish firm.

While HIMS pulled the drug from the market over the weekend due to regulatory scrutiny, its shares slumped ~16% on Monday as Novo (NVO) announced it filed a lawsuit alleging that the company violated a key patent related to semaglutide, the active ingredient of Wegovy.

However, short interest against Hims & Hers (HIMS) rose to a new 52-week high as of Feb. 11, financial data provider S3 Partners LLC said this week.

“Enthusiasm around weight loss offerings has given way to legal uncertainty following Novo Nordisk’s lawsuit targeting ‘copycat’ drugs,” Bloomberg reported, quoting S3 Partners managing director Matthew Unterman.

According to S3 Partners, HIMS's short interest has increased by ~46% over the past 12 months, to 77.5M shares (37% float), from 50.3M shares (28% float) as the stock plunged 60%, further intensifying the volatility.

Given its data on the options market, the firm projects a one-day move of +/-14% for HIMS after the company’s Q4 2025 results scheduled for Feb. 23.

data.title: Canada's Algoma Steel surges while U.S. peers plunge as Trump set to roll back some tariffs
data.description:
Algoma Steel (ASTL) +11.1% in Friday's trading after reports suggest that President Trump is considering rolling back steel and aluminum tariffs.

Trump’s 50% tariffs on the imported supplies have been a key measure in aiming to curb the flow of Chinese metal that has been pushing global prices lower.

The shift comes as U.S. Commerce Department officials believe the tariffs are hurting consumers by raising prices for goods containing metal products, such as auto parts and food and drink cans, the Financial Times reported.

Canadian steelmakers were not spared from Trump' tariffs, and now - unlike U.S. steelmakers - Algoma (ASTL) shares are surging on the report, although the stock is still down 45% from a year ago as it navigated pressures of tariffs and duties from its largest trading partner.

Among U.S. companies, Steel Dynamics (STLD) -3.4% and Nucor (NUE) -2.6%, ranking the stocks among the S&P 500's biggest decliners.

ETFs: (SLX), (XME)

data.title: Ingersoll Rand outlines 2026 revenue growth target of 2.5%-4.5% while advancing recurring revenue and M&A strategy
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Ingersoll Rand Inc. (IR) Q4 2025

Management View

  • Vicente Reynal, Chairman, CEO & President, opened the call highlighting "low single-digit organic order growth for both the fourth quarter and the full year," and a return to organic revenue growth, which he said "reflects positive momentum heading into 2026." Reynal also noted recurring revenue exceeded $450 million in 2025, with a backlog of approximately $1.1 billion from existing contracts, adding, "This is a clear demonstration of how we continue to make great progress towards achieving our recurring revenue targets."
  • Reynal emphasized M&A as a core growth driver, stating, "Our acquisition pipeline remains robust with a strategic focus on enhancing our existing portfolio." He detailed $525 million invested in 16 transactions in 2025, generating about $275 million in annualized inorganic revenue, and revealed that there are "9 additional transactions currently under LOI."
  • In January, IR completed the acquisition of Scinomix, advancing its Life Science strategy with workflow optimization technologies.
  • Vikram Kini, Senior VP & CFO, reported, "Orders showed continued strength in the fourth quarter, up 8% year-over-year or up 1% organically," with both ITS and PST segments delivering low single-digit organic order growth. Kini stated, "We delivered fourth quarter adjusted EBITDA of $580 million and adjusted EBITDA margins remained strong at 27.7%." Adjusted earnings per share for the quarter was $0.96, up 14% year-over-year. For the year, Kini said, "Adjusted earnings per share for the year was $3.34, up 2% year-over-year."

Outlook

  • The company issued its full year 2026 guidance: total revenue growth of 2.5% to 4.5%, driven by organic order growth of 1% at the midpoint, 1.5% growth from M&A (including the Scinomix acquisition and 2025 carryover), and a 1% FX tailwind.
  • Total adjusted EBITDA is expected in the range of $2.13 billion to $2.19 billion; corporate costs are planned at $170 million, spread evenly per quarter.
  • Adjusted EPS guidance is $3.45 to $3.57, with "approximately 5% growth at the midpoint." The adjusted tax rate is anticipated around 23%, net interest expense about $230 million, and share count near 394 million.
  • Reynal described the guidance as embedding "very stable sequentially here from what we are seeing today," and stated, "We're not embedding any market recovery here."

Financial Results

  • Fourth quarter revenue increased 10% year-over-year, with organic revenue up 3% and positive contributions from both price and volume.
  • For the full year, total revenue was up 6%, though organic revenue finished down 1% due to tough first half comps. Adjusted EBITDA for the year was approximately $2.1 billion, with a margin of 27.4%.
  • Free cash flow for Q4 was $462 million. The company reported $3.8 billion in total liquidity and leverage well under 2x, after deploying $525 million in M&A, $1 billion in share repurchases, and $32 million in dividends during 2025.
  • ITS segment delivered Q4 revenue growth of 11% year-over-year (3% organic), with adjusted EBITDA margin at 28.9%. PST segment saw Q4 revenue up 8% (4% organic), and adjusted EBITDA margin improved to 30.4%.

Q&A

  • Michael Halloran, Baird, asked about end market trajectory in guidance and short-cycle business signals. Reynal responded, "Portfolio continues to demonstrate resiliency... Life Sciences is progressing and improving sequentially," and noted strong funnel activity in both U.S. and China. He added, "We're not embedding any market recovery here, and very stable sequentially here from what we are seeing today."
  • Julian Mitchell, Barclays, queried seasonality and margin trends. Kini replied, "We expect Q1 organic to be... roughly flat to maybe very slightly down," with low single-digit growth for the rest of the year. On margins, Kini said, "We did expect some headwinds on the margin front, particularly in the first half of the year, particularly as we lap kind of some of the annualizing of the tariffs."
  • Jeffrey Sprague, Vertical Research, asked about short-cycle momentum and volume outlook. Reynal said, "We're seeing some pickup in the short cycle, clearly... order momentum, I'll say, continues." Kini noted, "We do expect volume performance to improve as we think about the second half versus the first half."
  • Joseph O'Dea, Wells Fargo, explored the M&A pipeline and recurring revenue. Reynal described the pipeline as "still as being bolt-on in nature," but with a few potential larger deals. On recurring revenue, he said the company has "now have approximately $1.1 billion in future revenue from existing contracts."
  • Nigel Coe, Wolfe Research, asked about Q1 deceleration and investment spending. Kini attributed Q4 to Q1 change to "normal seasonality," and described ongoing commercial investments for growth.
  • Multiple analysts probed margin profiles, Life Science momentum, China trends, and free cash flow conversion.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts maintained a neutral to slightly positive tone, with several expressing interest in growth catalysts, M&A, and segment dynamics. Questions sought clarification on guidance assumptions, margin headwinds, and market signals, reflecting cautious optimism but some skepticism about macro uncertainties.
  • Management's tone in prepared remarks was confident, emphasizing resilience, recurring revenue momentum, and M&A discipline. During Q&A, management was factual, at times reiterating prudence: "We're not embedding any market recovery here," and "We just want to see more data points of kind of continued better market performance."
  • Compared to the previous quarter, both analysts and management showed marginally increased optimism, particularly regarding China and Life Sciences, but continued prudence in guidance.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance language shifted from a "prudent view of Q4" in the prior quarter to a more stable outlook for 2026, with explicit commentary that no market recovery is assumed.
  • The recurring revenue milestone of $450 million and $1.1 billion backlog marks a step up from "approximately $200 million" previously mentioned, reinforcing progress on strategic targets.
  • Management's tone was more assured regarding M&A and recurring revenue execution, while analysts focused more deeply on segment trends and forward indicators, especially in Life Sciences and China.
  • Key metrics such as adjusted EBITDA and EPS showed year-over-year improvement, and both ITS and PST segments maintained or improved their margin profiles.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management highlighted tariff impacts and ongoing commercial investments as margin pressures, with Kini stating, "year-over-year margin pressure primarily driven by tariff impacts and intentional commercial investments for growth."
  • Guidance assumes no material market recovery, reflecting continued uncertainty in macroeconomic conditions.
  • Analysts probed for signs of market inflection, questioning the sustainability of recent order trends and the impact of seasonality, short-cycle, and long-cycle demand.
  • Working capital efficiency and inventory, particularly post-tariff, remain areas for improvement in free cash flow conversion.

Final Takeaway

Ingersoll Rand concluded 2025 with growth in recurring revenue, a robust M&A pipeline, and improved order trends across key segments. Management projects stable, modest revenue and EPS growth for 2026, guided by prudent assumptions and a resilient operating model. The company remains focused on disciplined capital deployment, innovation-driven margin expansion, and leveraging both organic and inorganic initiatives to drive long-term shareholder value.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript
